description: Imperative 'no' makes the following clause a directive, either a command or a request for action. It makes that force audible; whether the act is gentle still depends on who speaks to whom and under what circumstances. The separate particle 'pi' (POL) adds politeness in the order 'pi no'.
articulatory_notes: Set the tongue tip against the back surfaces of the upper teeth and keep voicing through the nose for dental 'n'. Release the contact, lower it, and add lip rounding for true-mid 'o'. Stress covers the whole particle.
usage_notes: An ordinary directive begins with 'no', and the verb keeps its usual form. The subject may be spoken when it matters. Adding 'pi' changes the courtesy, not the directive force.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: The particle puts directive force in the grammar rather than leaving it to tone. That clarity makes a command easier to recognise and answer, while leaving its ethics to the relationship and situation.
